statement: A clock pendulum of length L = 1.0 m is pulled to θ₀ = 1.4 rad (≈ 80.2°) and released. (a) Compute the small-angle period T₀. (b) Compute the exact period T_exact using the elliptic integral formula. (c) By what percentage does the true period exceed T₀? (d) After how many complete swings does the large-angle pendulum lag behind an identical small-angle pendulum by exactly one full period T₀?

commonMistakes: Using g = 9.8 instead of 9.80665 — acceptable for estimation but introduces a 0.007% error.

T_exact.prompt: Compute T_exact = 4√(L/g) · K(sin(θ₀/2)) for θ₀ = 1.4 rad.
commonMistakes: Applied the small-angle formula at 80° — the error is already 13.8%, invalidating any precision calculation.
Used a two-term series; at 1.4 rad the series error is already 0.18%, which matters for this problem.
Divided by T_exact instead of T₀ in the denominator — gives ≈ 12.1 %, a different quantity.
Reported the ratio T_exact/T₀ ≈ 1.138 without converting to a percentage.

n_cycles.prompt: After how many swings does the true pendulum lag the small-angle pendulum by exactly one period T₀? Use n = T₀ / (T_exact − T₀).
commonMistakes: Used n = T_exact / (T_exact − T₀) — this counts from T_exact, not T₀, as the reference.
Reported n in seconds rather than in cycles (dimensionless swing count).

walkthrough: At 80.2° the modulus k = sin(40.1°) ≈ 0.644. The elliptic integral K(0.644) ≈ 1.792, well above K(0) = π/2. The exact period is 4√(1/g)·K ≈ 2.284 s, stretching T₀ ≈ 2.006 s by 13.8%. The isochronism Galileo observed at small angles has completely broken down. Every cycle the large-angle pendulum lags the small-angle one by ΔT = 0.278 s. After n ≈ 7.2 swings the accumulated lag equals one full T₀, so the two pendulums are exactly one cycle out of phase — the large-angle one completes only 6.2 swings while the small-angle one does 7.2. This is the physical cost of ignoring sin θ ≈ θ at large amplitudes.
